MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jul-28-2022 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Oct-18-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MANDATORY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-04-2022 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jun-30-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BASILIO PRIETO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

PacPizza, LLC and Basilio Flores Prieto's summary judgment motion is denied.

In September 2018, plaintiffs Maria Keehn and Eric Thorne were injured when struck in a crosswalk at high speed. (PRDSS 1; Memo. 1:4.) They were hit by a vehicle that Miguel Barbonier drove and Mirka Del Castillo owned. (PRDSS 2.) Prieto allegedly bore fault for the accident by failing to yield to the Barbonier-driven vehicle. (PSS 1.) Prieto was apparently working for PacPizza.

In February 2019, plaintiffs settled bodily injury claims with the impecunious Barbonier and Del Castillo for policy limits. (PRDSS 3-4; PSS 6-8.) Plaintiffs signed "bodily injury only" releases of Del Castillo, Barbonier, their agents and others, including "any other person" responsible for plaintiffs' injuries. (PRDSS 3-5.) Neither Prieto nor PacPizza is named in those releases. (Id.) Later in 2019, plaintiffs released property damage claims. (PSS 13-14.) This time, the releases named Prieto and PacPizza along with Barbonier and Del Castillo. (Id.)

PacPizza and Prieto move for summary judgment claiming to be third-party-beneficiary "other persons" under the bodily injury releases.

I find triable issues of fact as to whether the bodily injury releases were intended to benefit Prieto and PacPizza. (See Hess v. Ford Motor Co. (2002) 27 Cal.4th 516, 524; CCP 877(a) ("release... shall not discharge any other such party from liability unless its terms so provide").) Prieto and PacPizza were not involved in negotiating the bodily injury releases and are not named in them. (PSS 11.) Why plaintiffs would release insured and seemingly culpable defendants without payment from them is not explained. Sloppy drafting is the logical inference. This conclusion is fortified by the inclusion of Prieto and PacPizza in the property damage releases, where it actually was intended that they be released. (PSS 13-14.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/RBU)

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Sep-16-2021 Continued To Nov-03-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR DETERMINATION OF GOOD FAITH SETTLEMENT, ETC.
Off Calendar Aug-11-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Jennifer Sommer, Esq. (Jennifer.Sommer@csaa.com) of Carbone, Smith & Koyama for Moving Party/Cross-Defendant Mirka Del Castillo.
Christopher Nolin, Esq. (415-421-3100 x1017; Nolin@gkhs.com) of Gordon-Creed, Kelley, Holl & Sugerman, LLP for Defendant, PacPizza, LLC.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT MIRKA DEL CASTILLO's MOTION FOR DETERMINATION OF GOOD FAITH SETTLEMENT, ETC.

Cross-Defendant Mirka Del Castillo and Miguel Barbonier's motion for good faith determination is denied without prejudice. In Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499, the California Supreme Court held the following factors are relevant in evaluating the good faith of a settlement: (1) a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability; (2) the amount paid in settlement; (3) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; (4) a recognition that a settlor should pay less in settlement than he would if he were found liable after a trial; (5) the financial conditions of the settling defendants, as well as the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants; and (6) defendant's settlement figure must not be grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person, at the time of the settlement, would estimate the settling defendant's liability to be. "The ultimate determinant of good faith is whether the settlement is grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person at the time of settlement would estimate the settlor's liability to be." (City of Grand Terrace v. Superior Court (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 1251, 1262.)

Here, there is insufficient evidence to enable the Court to roughly approximate plaintiffs' total recovery and settlors' proportionate fault to determine whether the proposed settlement is in good faith. Indeed, moving parties do not provide any information from which the Court could make a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery. Rather, their showing establishes only that plaintiffs, injured pedestrians, allege unspecified wage loss, property damage, medical expenses, loss of earning capacity, and general damages. That is insufficient. (See also Greshko v. County of Los Angeles (1987) 194 Cal.App.3d 822, 834 [conclusory attorney affidavits are not substantial evidence to support a finding of a good faith settlement].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
